title: Emergency system-test fixes
body:
Emergency fix to image-scp tests. DO NOT CREATE A USER!
These tests are run in all sorts of environments. We
do not have the right to vandalize a production system.

Also remove some misleading unneeded tests; and refactor a
little; and add a bunch of FIXMEs which will need to be
addressed later.

Also, super-low priority, add 'crun: ' to expected error
message in a run test (minor followup to #12758).

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Is there a way to test this without creating a user? Do the system tests have multi user setups by default? The rootful test needs a user to transfer to

I wonder if there is something we could do with systemd dynamic users.
https://0pointer.net/blog/dynamic-users-with-systemd.html
also @edsantiago I think in the scp PR we made it so that we were added to the sudoers file so checking for sudo is not necessary. I am not sure though.

@cdoern I owe you a heap of apologies: first, for not catching this in review; second for the emergency nature of my PR last night. I only caught it by accident, and really had only minutes to submit my emergency PR (which was critical before cutting an RC). But I could've taken a few more minutes to spell out the problems in greater detail. I communicated poorly, and I am sorry.

The key concept is: system tests run in a whole lotta places. Not just our CI, also Fedora and CentOS and RHEL gating, and also a custom setup from some multiarch people, and also my own laptop, and also random people around the world, some of whom we're aware of, and probably many we're not. We can't go creating user accounts on all these systems. We also can't assume that `sudo` works on all these environments. (Correction: we can safely assume that sudo _does not_ work on some.)

This is going to be tricky to fix: it will need some handshaking between the underlying infrastructure and the tests themselves. Perhaps an envariable, or a state file, or something such that the tests can safely find out information about the context in which they're running. Nothing obvious came to mind last night, nor right now, but I'm sure we will come up with something next week

I am very sorry for not taking the time to write this out last night. And, as one of the original requesters of this functionality, I'm truly thankful for your work!

@edsantiago no worries at all, I'm sorry I didn't think about the wider scope of the system tests when writing these. I think with some research this week I will be able to make a more contained yet effective test for scp. I will tag you in my future PRs containing system tests just to make sure I am staying within the bounds of what is legal! Thanks for catching this and for explaining what the issue was!
